text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE

Clinic Date: 28 September 2016

Dr Wang
Saffron Park Hospital

Saffron Park, London, E14 7JL
Dear Dr Wang
John Doe, DOB: 21-11-1982, Hospital No: P546484 NHS No. 5484656746
Flat 15 Roundwood Road, London, E14 7JL

Thank you for asking me to review your patient in the Neurology Clinic. He is known to have generalised epilepsy and remains under long-term antiseizure therapy with bone-health monitoring recorded elsewhere in his notes. I last reviewed him three months ago when we simplified his regimen to improve daytime alertness and reduce cumulative adverse effects.

Since that visit, there has been a marked change in his day-to-day seizure pattern. Following the recent medication adjustment, focal impaired-awareness seizures have been occurring more often, three - four times per day, whereas previously they only happened once or twice a month. He describes brief episodes of behavioural arrest with staring, loss of awareness and post-event confusion lasting 5–10 minutes, occasionally followed by headache. There have been no generalised tonic–clonic seizures reported over the past six weeks. He denies new injuries, tongue biting or incontinence. Triggers noted include sleep deprivation and missed meals; alcohol intake is minimal. Family members have recorded typical events on a smartphone, which corroborate the semiology and frequency he reports.

Current medicines (patient-reported): lamotrigine 200 mg b.d. and levetiracetam 1 g b.d. The adjustment three months ago involved tapering and discontinuation of topiramate over four weeks. He reports improved clarity of thinking since then, but the increase in focal impaired-awareness events started soon after the taper. No rash, mood instability or gastrointestinal intolerance reported. He drives neither a car nor a motorcycle.

Examination today is unremarkable with normal cardiorespiratory and focused neurological assessment. Observations are stable. Recent routine bloods from primary care, including FBC, U&Es and LFTs, are within reference limits as per shared results. Bone-health surveillance and vitamin D monitoring are documented in his long-term therapy record held elsewhere.

Plan discussed with the patient:
- Diagnostic clarification: although he is known to have generalised epilepsy, the current increase in focal impaired-awareness episodes following the medication change warrants repeat EEG with hyperventilation and photic stimulation, and consideration of ambulatory EEG to assess interictal generalised discharges versus focal onset features.
- Safety: reinforced seizure first-aid and avoidance of heights, open water and unsupervised bathing. Driving advice unchanged (not driving).
- Medications: no emergent adverse effects reported; we agreed not to make further changes today pending EEG and review of smartphone event videos in clinic.
- Follow-up: clinic review in 6–8 weeks, earlier if events escalate or if a generalised tonic–clonic seizure occurs.

I will update you once the EEG has been completed and we have reviewed the recordings with him. Please let me know if there is any additional information from primary care or if issues arise before his next appointment.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: focal impaired-awareness seizures have been occurring more often, three - four times per day
Following the recent medication adjustment, focal impaired-awareness seizures have been occurring more often, three - four times per day, whereas previously they only happened once or twice a month

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Identify explicit current seizure frequency. The letter states: 'Following the recent medication adjustment, focal impaired-awareness seizures have been occurring more often, three - four times per day...' This is a clear numeric range of current events. No generalised tonic–clonic seizures in past six weeks does not negate the frequent focal events. Apply rule to pick highest frequency across types; only one type has frequency specified, so use '3 to 4 per day'. Ensure formatting uses 'to' and unit 'day'. Not seizure free because ongoing daily events.
seizure_frequency_number: focal impaired-awareness seizures have been occurring more often, three - four times per day
reference: Following the recent medication adjustment, focal impaired-awareness seizures have been occurring more often, three - four times per day, whereas previously they only happened once or twice a month
